message.content: Masquerading: Space after Filename is a technique where adversaries manipulate file extensions to disguise malicious executables. By appending a space to the end of a filename, the operating system processes the file based on its true type rather than the apparent extension. For instance, renaming a Mach-O executable from "evil.bin" to "evil.txt " (with a trailing space) will cause the OS to execute the binary instead of opening it with a text editor. This method can deceive users into executing harmful files that appear benign. Commonly exploited by malware like Keydnap, this technique leverages user trust and OS behavior to facilitate execution. Mitigation strategies include user education on file extensions, disabling execution of files with suspicious extensions, and employing endpoint security solutions to detect and block such masquerading attempts.
